reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Documented model calls for a unified 8-year primary school with no selective entry (Hogyan legyünk okos nemzet?, 2021); this implies the existence of an early selection point itself, not merely its coachability, is the problem to be solved.
reactions.interest: children currently excluded from selective tracks by SES background, and the general-school pupils left behind by the departure of high-achieving peers
reactions.public_good_frame: an 'okos nemzet' (smart nation) requires efficient use of the whole talent pool, which early, SES-skewed sorting undermines regardless of how technically fair the sorting instrument is
reactions.argument: Replacing the coachable exam with a less-coachable assessment and adding SES quotas may improve who is selected among applicants, but it leaves the selection point at age 10/12 itself intact — our documented model does not call for fairer gatekeeping, it calls for removing the gate. A reform that makes early selection more defensible without shrinking or removing it treats a symptom rather than the mechanism the book identifies as the source of inequity.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would move toward support if S1 were explicitly legislated as a transitional step with a statutory pathway or sunset toward eventual structural unification (as in S2/S3); we would move toward opposition if S1 is presented as a durable end-state that forecloses structural reform by making the current tracks appear sufficiently fair.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Documented preference for a unified 8-year primary school with no selective entry implies support for any credible trajectory that reduces selective-entry places toward zero, since the end-state described matches our documented model even where the glide-path mechanics themselves are not addressed in the source text.
reactions.interest: children currently excluded from selective tracks by SES background, and general schools seeking a credible trajectory of rising capacity and status
reactions.public_good_frame: a smart nation is built by phasing sorting later and investing the freed capacity into strong general schools, rather than by permanently ring-fencing a minority selective track
reactions.argument: A pre-announced statutory cap trajectory that shrinks selective-entry places each year moves in the direction of our documented end-state — a unified primary school without early selective entry — even though our source text does not specify a glide path, timeline, or cap percentages. The direction of travel matches; the specific mechanism does not need to be documented for us to recognize it as consistent with our position.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support strengthens if the trajectory is legally entrenched against reversal and reaches zero selective places on a firm schedule; support weakens toward opposition if the caps stall indefinitely at a nonzero level, since a permanently shrunken but still-selective track is not the unified model we document, only a smaller version of the structure we oppose.

reactions.label: documented
reactions.interest: children currently excluded from selective tracks by SES background, and the national talent pool as a whole
reactions.public_good_frame: a unified lower-secondary system without early selective entry is, per our documented model, the structural precondition for building a genuinely 'smart nation' that does not waste talent through early, SES-linked sorting
reactions.argument: Converting 6-/8-year gimnázium entry points into upper-secondary-only institutions and moving first selection to age 14 is the structural change our 2021 publication documents as necessary: a unified primary school with no selective entry before that age. This scenario matches our documented model directly, not merely in spirit.
reactions.condition_to_change: Our support would weaken, though not reverse, if implementation evidence showed general schools consistently failed to absorb and challenge high-achieving pupils even under adequate funding and retraining, since our documented model assumes comprehensive schools can deliver this given proper investment; persistent failure on that point would require us to revisit the model's implementation assumptions, though not the underlying no-early-selection position itself.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Documented model calls for eliminating the early selection point via a unified 8-year primary school with no selective entry; a scenario that explicitly retains the 6-/8-year selective structure and compensates its effects afterward conflicts with this documented end-state.
reactions.interest: children who would otherwise be sorted away from high-achieving peers at age 10/12, and general schools whose disadvantage originates from that sorting
reactions.public_good_frame: compensating for the downstream effects of early selection does not resolve the structural inefficiency the book identifies — a smart nation needs the sorting mechanism itself addressed, not just its symptoms mitigated
reactions.argument: This scenario retains the exact selection point our documented model calls for eliminating, and instead directs resources at mitigating its consequences after the fact. Beyond being structurally insufficient, by making the current tracked structure more fiscally and politically tolerable it risks entrenching early selection permanently — precisely the outcome our documented model argues against — while leaving compensation funding exposed to being cut in future fiscal cycles, per this scenario's own political-risk analysis.
reactions.condition_to_change: We would soften our opposition if the compensation package were explicitly legislated as a bounded, transitional bridge toward eventual structural unification (e.g., paired with a sunset clause or phased cap as in S2), rather than presented as a standalone, indefinite substitute for structural reform.
